name: Italy
summary: Italy ranges from cool northern regions to warm Mediterranean south, with major regional diversity and many important indigenous grapes.

alto-adige-trentino-friuli.name: Alto Adige / Trentino / Friuli-Venezia Giulia
alto-adige-trentino-friuli.summary: Cooler north-eastern Italian zone influenced by altitude and mountains, producing fresh aromatic whites and elegant reds.
alto-adige-trentino-friuli.styleSummary: Fresh aromatic whites and some light to medium-bodied reds, often with good acidity.
examTips: Think cooler north-eastern Italy with freshness and acidity.
Altitude and Alpine influence are key themes.
Pinot Grigio is especially important here.

Pinot Grigio.summary: Fresh light to medium-bodied white grape producing pear and citrus styles in cooler sites.
Pinot Grigio.style: Light to medium-bodied white with pear, citrus and subtle stone fruit notes.
examTips: Usually fresher and more precise here than in hotter regions.

Sauvignon Blanc.summary: Aromatic white grape producing citrus, herb and green fruit notes with good freshness.
Sauvignon Blanc.style: Fresh aromatic whites with citrus, herb and green fruit notes.
winemaking: Usually stainless steel to preserve aromatics
examTips: Think freshness and aromatic purity.

Chardonnay.summary: Versatile white grape showing fresh citrus and stone fruit, sometimes with subtle oak.
Chardonnay.style: Fresh citrus to stone-fruit styles, sometimes with subtle oak.
examTips: Usually fresher and lighter than richer warm-climate Chardonnay.

vineyardFactors: Moderate conditions generally allow steady ripening while still retaining freshness and balance.
Continental conditions usually mean a wider seasonal and day-night temperature range, with frost and winter cold often relevant.
Altitude or large diurnal range helps preserve acidity and aromatic definition even where sunshine is strong.
Mountain influence or rain-shadow effects can reduce rainfall and create cooler nights or sheltered mesoclimates.
humanFactors: Growers can use site and harvest decisions to balance reliable ripening with freshness and style objectives.
Variety selection, frost protection, and matching the best exposures to the right grapes are important in continental regions.
Site selection by elevation and harvest timing are major human choices in these vineyards.
Growers rely on site selection and exposure to exploit shelter and reduce climatic risk.

veneto.name: Veneto
veneto.summary: Large north-eastern Italian region producing a wide range of styles including Valpolicella reds, Soave whites and Prosecco sparkling wines.
veneto.styleSummary: Wide range including reds, Soave whites and fresh tank method sparkling Prosecco.
examTips: Veneto is diverse, so link grape to style and subregion.
Corvina is key for Valpolicella and Amarone-related styles.
Garganega is central to Soave and Glera to Prosecco.

Corvina.summary: Key red grape of Veneto, giving red cherry fruit, freshness and medium body, especially in Valpolicella styles.
Corvina.style: Red cherry, medium body and fresh acidity in Valpolicella styles.
winemaking: Fresh styles or appassimento-based richer styles
examTips: Think red cherry and freshness in basic styles.
Also important in appassimento-based richer wines.

Garganega.summary: Main white grape of Soave, producing medium-bodied citrus and almond-accented wines.
Garganega.style: Medium-bodied white with citrus, pear and almond notes.
winemaking: Can be simple or more textured
examTips: Remember Soave = Garganega.

Pinot Grigio.summary: Widely planted white grape usually made in a light fresh pear-and-citrus style.
Pinot Grigio.style: Light to medium-bodied white with pear and citrus notes.
examTips: Usually light, fresh and straightforward rather than highly aromatic.

Glera.summary: The key grape of Prosecco, producing light-bodied aromatic sparkling wines with pear and apple fruit.
Glera.style: Pear, apple, floral notes and light body in fresh tank method sparkling wines.
viticulture: Best on cooler hills for freshness
winemaking: Usually made in youthful fruit-driven styles
examTips: Tank method preserves primary fruit.
Usually lighter and simpler than traditional method sparkling wine.

prosecco-doc.name: Prosecco DOC
prosecco-doc.style: Fresh, light, fruity tank method sparkling wine with pear and apple notes.
prosecco-doc.aging: No extended lees ageing; usually released young
prosecco-doc.keyPoint: Primary fruit and frothy freshness are the key clues.

conegliano-valdobbiadene-prosecco-superi.name: Conegliano Valdobbiadene Prosecco Superiore DOCG
conegliano-valdobbiadene-prosecco-superi.style: More concentrated, finer and often more complex Prosecco from better hill sites.
conegliano-valdobbiadene-prosecco-superi.aging: Tank method, usually short ageing to preserve fruit
conegliano-valdobbiadene-prosecco-superi.keyPoint: Higher quality zone with steeper hill vineyards.

amarone-della-valpolicella.name: Amarone della Valpolicella
amarone-della-valpolicella.style: Full-bodied dry red with concentrated dried-fruit character from appassimento grapes.
amarone-della-valpolicella.aging: Appassimento followed by fermentation and maturation
amarone-della-valpolicella.keyPoint: Know the appassimento method and dry finished style.

valpolicella.name: Valpolicella
valpolicella.summary: Important Veneto red wine zone producing Valpolicella and the dried-grape Amarone and Recioto styles.
valpolicella.styleSummary: Cherry-fruited reds from fresh Valpolicella through richer Amarone-based styles.
examTips: Know this as the home of Amarone and Ripasso.
Appassimento is a key technique here.

Corvina.summary: Key red grape of Veneto, giving red cherry fruit, freshness and medium body, especially in Valpolicella styles.
Corvina.style: Red cherry, medium body and fresh acidity in Valpolicella styles.
winemaking: Fresh styles or appassimento-based richer styles
examTips: Think red cherry and freshness in basic styles.
Also important in appassimento-based richer wines.

valpolicella.classification: Valpolicella covers fresh red styles plus the dried-grape Amarone and sweet Recioto traditions.
lawNotes: Ripasso and Amarone are key exam concepts here.
vineyardFactors: Moderate conditions generally allow steady ripening while still retaining freshness and balance.
Continental conditions usually mean a wider seasonal and day-night temperature range, with frost and winter cold often relevant.
Rivers or lakes can moderate temperature, reflect light, and shape the best vineyard exposures.
Mountain influence or rain-shadow effects can reduce rainfall and create cooler nights or sheltered mesoclimates.
humanFactors: Growers can use site and harvest decisions to balance reliable ripening with freshness and style objectives.
Variety selection, frost protection, and matching the best exposures to the right grapes are important in continental regions.
Growers rely on site selection and exposure to exploit shelter and reduce climatic risk.
Choice of the best grape varieties and exact site exposure is an important human factor in this sub-region.

soave.name: Soave
soave.summary: Classic Veneto white wine area east of Verona known for Garganega-based dry wines.
soave.styleSummary: Dry medium-bodied whites with pear, citrus and almond notes, often with better concentration from hillside sites.
examTips: Better sites on hills can give more concentration and ageing ability.
Garganega is the key grape to remember.

Garganega.summary: Main white grape of Soave, producing medium-bodied citrus and almond-accented wines.
Garganega.style: Medium-bodied white with citrus, pear and almond notes.
winemaking: Can be simple or more textured
examTips: Remember Soave = Garganega.

soave.classification: Soave is the classic Garganega-based white appellation, with better hillside sites important for quality.
vineyardFactors: Moderate conditions generally allow steady ripening while still retaining freshness and balance.
Continental conditions usually mean a wider seasonal and day-night temperature range, with frost and winter cold often relevant.
Rivers or lakes can moderate temperature, reflect light, and shape the best vineyard exposures.
Mountain influence or rain-shadow effects can reduce rainfall and create cooler nights or sheltered mesoclimates.
humanFactors: Growers can use site and harvest decisions to balance reliable ripening with freshness and style objectives.
Variety selection, frost protection, and matching the best exposures to the right grapes are important in continental regions.
Growers rely on site selection and exposure to exploit shelter and reduce climatic risk.
Choice of the best grape varieties and exact site exposure is an important human factor in this sub-region.

conegliano-valdobbiadene.name: Conegliano-Valdobbiadene
conegliano-valdobbiadene.summary: Premium hillside Prosecco area producing more complex and concentrated sparkling wines than the broader DOC.
conegliano-valdobbiadene.styleSummary: Aromatic sparkling wines with pear, peach and floral notes, often with finer quality and more intensity.
examTips: This is the premium Prosecco Superiore zone.
Steep hillside vineyards are an important quality clue.

Glera.summary: The key grape of Prosecco, producing light-bodied aromatic sparkling wines with pear and apple fruit.
Glera.style: Pear, apple, floral notes and light body in fresh tank method sparkling wines.
viticulture: Best on cooler hills for freshness
winemaking: Usually made in youthful fruit-driven styles
examTips: Tank method preserves primary fruit.
Usually lighter and simpler than traditional method sparkling wine.

conegliano-valdobbiadene.classification: Conegliano-Valdobbiadene Prosecco Superiore DOCG is above the broader Prosecco DOC in the quality hierarchy.
vineyardFactors: Moderate conditions generally allow steady ripening while still retaining freshness and balance.
Continental conditions usually mean a wider seasonal and day-night temperature range, with frost and winter cold often relevant.
Rivers or lakes can moderate temperature, reflect light, and shape the best vineyard exposures.
Mountain influence or rain-shadow effects can reduce rainfall and create cooler nights or sheltered mesoclimates.
humanFactors: Growers can use site and harvest decisions to balance reliable ripening with freshness and style objectives.
Variety selection, frost protection, and matching the best exposures to the right grapes are important in continental regions.
Growers rely on site selection and exposure to exploit shelter and reduce climatic risk.
Choice of the best grape varieties and exact site exposure is an important human factor in this sub-region.

veneto.classification: Veneto spans key DOC and DOCG names including Soave, Valpolicella and premium Prosecco zones.
lawNotes: Amarone and Recioto rely on appassimento.
Conegliano-Valdobbiadene Prosecco Superiore is the prestige Prosecco area.
vineyardFactors: Moderate conditions generally allow steady ripening while still retaining freshness and balance.
Continental conditions usually mean a wider seasonal and day-night temperature range, with frost and winter cold often relevant.
Mediterranean conditions usually bring abundant sunshine and relatively dry growing seasons, helping consistent ripening.
Rivers or lakes can moderate temperature, reflect light, and shape the best vineyard exposures.
humanFactors: Growers can use site and harvest decisions to balance reliable ripening with freshness and style objectives.
Variety selection, frost protection, and matching the best exposures to the right grapes are important in continental regions.
Water management, shade from canopy, and choosing cooler or higher sites are important human responses in Mediterranean climates.
Growers rely on site selection and exposure to exploit shelter and reduce climatic risk.

tuscany.name: Tuscany
tuscany.summary: Classic central Italian region centred on Sangiovese, producing savoury reds with freshness and regional variation.
tuscany.styleSummary: Sangiovese-led reds with freshness and savoury character.
examTips: Sangiovese is the core grape of Tuscany.
Think sour cherry, herbs and savoury structure.
Altitude and hillside sites help preserve freshness.

Sangiovese.summary: Italy's key central red grape, known for sour cherry fruit, savoury notes and fresh acidity.
Sangiovese.style: Sour cherry, herbs, savoury notes with fresh acidity.
viticulture: Needs sufficient warmth but not excessive heat
examTips: High acidity plus sour cherry is a key clue.
Usually more savoury than plush.

Cabernet Sauvignon.summary: International variety used in richer structured Tuscan styles and blends.
Cabernet Sauvignon.style: Black fruit, cassis and firmer structure in international styles.
examTips: Used in more international-style Tuscan wines.

Merlot.summary: Plum-fruited softer-textured international grape used in Tuscan blends and varietal wines.
Merlot.style: Plum, black fruit and rounder texture.
examTips: Usually softer and rounder than Cabernet Sauvignon.

chianti-classico.name: Chianti Classico
chianti-classico.summary: Historic central Tuscan zone between Florence and Siena focused on quality Sangiovese-based reds.
chianti-classico.styleSummary: Sour cherry, red plum and savory Sangiovese reds with firm tannin and high acidity.
examTips: Higher acidity and tannin are classic Sangiovese clues.
Altitude helps preserve freshness here.

Sangiovese.summary: Italy's key central red grape, known for sour cherry fruit, savoury notes and fresh acidity.
Sangiovese.style: Sour cherry, herbs, savoury notes with fresh acidity.
viticulture: Needs sufficient warmth but not excessive heat
examTips: High acidity plus sour cherry is a key clue.
Usually more savoury than plush.

chianti-classico.classification: Chianti Classico DOCG is distinct from the broader Chianti DOCG and uses the black rooster as an important symbol.
vineyardFactors: Warmer conditions increase sugar accumulation and body, so retaining acidity and avoiding over-ripeness become more important.
Mediterranean conditions usually bring abundant sunshine and relatively dry growing seasons, helping consistent ripening.
Altitude lowers temperature and can extend the growing season, helping preserve acidity and aroma.
Altitude or large diurnal range helps preserve acidity and aromatic definition even where sunshine is strong.
humanFactors: Harvest timing, canopy management, and site choice are important tools for keeping balance in warmer areas.
Water management, shade from canopy, and choosing cooler or higher sites are important human responses in Mediterranean climates.
Growers often use elevation and harvest timing strategically to balance ripeness and freshness.
Site selection by elevation and harvest timing are major human choices in these vineyards.

montalcino.name: Montalcino
montalcino.summary: Warm Tuscan hill town producing Brunello di Montalcino from Sangiovese with greater power and structure.
montalcino.styleSummary: Concentrated, structured and ageworthy Sangiovese with ripe cherry fruit and savory complexity.
examTips: Brunello is 100% Sangiovese and more powerful than many Chianti wines.
Warm conditions help fuller ripeness.

Sangiovese.summary: Italy's key central red grape, known for sour cherry fruit, savoury notes and fresh acidity.
Sangiovese.style: Sour cherry, herbs, savoury notes with fresh acidity.
viticulture: Needs sufficient warmth but not excessive heat
examTips: High acidity plus sour cherry is a key clue.
Usually more savoury than plush.

montalcino.classification: Brunello di Montalcino DOCG is made from 100% Sangiovese and requires long ageing.
vineyardFactors: Warmer conditions increase sugar accumulation and body, so retaining acidity and avoiding over-ripeness become more important.
Mediterranean conditions usually bring abundant sunshine and relatively dry growing seasons, helping consistent ripening.
Altitude or large diurnal range helps preserve acidity and aromatic definition even where sunshine is strong.
Slope, aspect, and drainage strongly affect ripening, frost risk, and vineyard quality.
humanFactors: Harvest timing, canopy management, and site choice are important tools for keeping balance in warmer areas.
Water management, shade from canopy, and choosing cooler or higher sites are important human responses in Mediterranean climates.
Site selection by elevation and harvest timing are major human choices in these vineyards.
Terracing, manual work, and careful site matching become more important on steep or exposed slopes.

montepulciano.name: Montepulciano
montepulciano.summary: Tuscan hill town known for Vino Nobile di Montepulciano, a Sangiovese-based red with ripe fruit and structure.
montepulciano.styleSummary: Medium to full-bodied Sangiovese-based reds with red cherry, plum and spice.
examTips: Do not confuse the town Montepulciano with the grape Montepulciano.
Vino Nobile is based on Sangiovese here.

Sangiovese.summary: Italy's key central red grape, known for sour cherry fruit, savoury notes and fresh acidity.
Sangiovese.style: Sour cherry, herbs, savoury notes with fresh acidity.
viticulture: Needs sufficient warmth but not excessive heat
examTips: High acidity plus sour cherry is a key clue.
Usually more savoury than plush.

montepulciano.classification: Vino Nobile di Montepulciano DOCG is based mainly on Sangiovese grown around the town of Montepulciano.
vineyardFactors: Warmer conditions increase sugar accumulation and body, so retaining acidity and avoiding over-ripeness become more important.
Mediterranean conditions usually bring abundant sunshine and relatively dry growing seasons, helping consistent ripening.
Altitude or large diurnal range helps preserve acidity and aromatic definition even where sunshine is strong.
Slope, aspect, and drainage strongly affect ripening, frost risk, and vineyard quality.
humanFactors: Harvest timing, canopy management, and site choice are important tools for keeping balance in warmer areas.
Water management, shade from canopy, and choosing cooler or higher sites are important human responses in Mediterranean climates.
Site selection by elevation and harvest timing are major human choices in these vineyards.
Terracing, manual work, and careful site matching become more important on steep or exposed slopes.

bolgheri.name: Bolgheri
bolgheri.summary: Coastal Tuscan appellation known for Bordeaux-variety blends in a warmer maritime setting.
bolgheri.styleSummary: Riper fuller-bodied reds based on Cabernet Sauvignon and Merlot with oak and international styling.
examTips: Think coastal Tuscany and Bordeaux varieties.
This is a key Super Tuscan area.

Cabernet Sauvignon.summary: International variety used in richer structured Tuscan styles and blends.
Cabernet Sauvignon.style: Black fruit, cassis and firmer structure in international styles.
examTips: Used in more international-style Tuscan wines.

Merlot.summary: Plum-fruited softer-textured international grape used in Tuscan blends and varietal wines.
Merlot.style: Plum, black fruit and rounder texture.
examTips: Usually softer and rounder than Cabernet Sauvignon.

bolgheri.classification: Bolgheri became one of the best-known homes of Super Tuscan Bordeaux-variety wines.
vineyardFactors: Warmer conditions increase sugar accumulation and body, so retaining acidity and avoiding over-ripeness become more important.
Maritime influence can increase humidity, rainfall, and vintage variation while helping moderate temperature extremes.
Altitude or large diurnal range helps preserve acidity and aromatic definition even where sunshine is strong.
Slope, aspect, and drainage strongly affect ripening, frost risk, and vineyard quality.
humanFactors: Harvest timing, canopy management, and site choice are important tools for keeping balance in warmer areas.
Disease control and canopy management are often important human factors in maritime climates.
Site selection by elevation and harvest timing are major human choices in these vineyards.
Terracing, manual work, and careful site matching become more important on steep or exposed slopes.

tuscany.classification: Key Tuscan quality names include Chianti Classico DOCG, Brunello di Montalcino DOCG, Vino Nobile di Montepulciano DOCG and the IGT Toscana category for many Super Tuscan wines.
lawNotes: Sangiovese is the central grape for the classic Tuscan DOCG wines.
IGT Toscana became important for non-traditional blends and varietal choices.
vineyardFactors: Moderate conditions generally allow steady ripening while still retaining freshness and balance.
Mediterranean conditions usually bring abundant sunshine and relatively dry growing seasons, helping consistent ripening.
Altitude or large diurnal range helps preserve acidity and aromatic definition even where sunshine is strong.
Slope, aspect, and drainage strongly affect ripening, frost risk, and vineyard quality.
humanFactors: Growers can use site and harvest decisions to balance reliable ripening with freshness and style objectives.
Water management, shade from canopy, and choosing cooler or higher sites are important human responses in Mediterranean climates.
Site selection by elevation and harvest timing are major human choices in these vineyards.
Terracing, manual work, and careful site matching become more important on steep or exposed slopes.

marche.name: Marche
marche.summary: Adriatic-facing central Italian region best known for fresh, characterful Verdicchio.
marche.styleSummary: Fresh whites, especially Verdicchio, with some elegant reds.
examTips: Verdicchio is the key grape here.
Think freshness, citrus and almond note.

Verdicchio.summary: Fresh central Italian white grape producing citrus, green apple and almond-accented wines with good acidity.
Verdicchio.style: Fresh citrus, green apple and sometimes almond notes, with good acidity.
viticulture: Performs well in Marche hillside sites
examTips: Almond note is a useful clue.
Usually fresher and more characterful than neutral Italian whites.

vineyardFactors: Moderate conditions generally allow steady ripening while still retaining freshness and balance.
Mediterranean conditions usually bring abundant sunshine and relatively dry growing seasons, helping consistent ripening.
Altitude or large diurnal range helps preserve acidity and aromatic definition even where sunshine is strong.
humanFactors: Growers can use site and harvest decisions to balance reliable ripening with freshness and style objectives.
Water management, shade from canopy, and choosing cooler or higher sites are important human responses in Mediterranean climates.
Site selection by elevation and harvest timing are major human choices in these vineyards.
Matching grape varieties and rootstocks to the best sites is an important human factor behind regional wine quality.

umbria.name: Umbria
umbria.summary: Inland central Italian region producing traditional whites and some structured reds from hilly sites.
umbria.styleSummary: Whites based around local and traditional grapes, plus some quality reds.
examTips: Think inland central Italy with traditional white blends.
Grechetto is the most useful grape to remember.

Grechetto.summary: Medium-bodied Umbrian white grape with citrus, orchard fruit and some texture.
Grechetto.style: Medium-bodied white with citrus, orchard fruit and some texture.
winemaking: Usually blended or varietal dry whites
examTips: More textural and characterful than very neutral whites.

Trebbiano.summary: Neutral productive white grape often used in blends for light dry wines.
Trebbiano.style: Neutral to lightly citrus white, often used in blends.
winemaking: Often used in simple dry styles
examTips: Usually more neutral than Grechetto.

vineyardFactors: Moderate conditions generally allow steady ripening while still retaining freshness and balance.
Continental conditions usually mean a wider seasonal and day-night temperature range, with frost and winter cold often relevant.
Mediterranean conditions usually bring abundant sunshine and relatively dry growing seasons, helping consistent ripening.
Altitude or large diurnal range helps preserve acidity and aromatic definition even where sunshine is strong.
humanFactors: Growers can use site and harvest decisions to balance reliable ripening with freshness and style objectives.
Variety selection, frost protection, and matching the best exposures to the right grapes are important in continental regions.
Water management, shade from canopy, and choosing cooler or higher sites are important human responses in Mediterranean climates.
Site selection by elevation and harvest timing are major human choices in these vineyards.

lazio.name: Lazio
lazio.summary: Central Italian region near Rome known mainly for traditional white blends.
lazio.styleSummary: Mainly white wines from traditional local blends.
examTips: Think simple to moderate white wines from traditional blends.
Volcanic soils can be relevant in some zones.

Malvasia.summary: Traditional aromatic to semi-aromatic white grape often used in Lazio blends.
Malvasia.style: Aromatic to neutral white, often medium-bodied with soft fruit notes.
examTips: Usually softer and broader than crisp high-acid whites.

Trebbiano.summary: Widely planted neutral white grape used for light dry blends.
Trebbiano.style: Light, neutral to citrus white used widely in blends.
examTips: Think neutral supporting grape rather than aromatic star.

vineyardFactors: Warmer conditions increase sugar accumulation and body, so retaining acidity and avoiding over-ripeness become more important.
Mediterranean conditions usually bring abundant sunshine and relatively dry growing seasons, helping consistent ripening.
Nearby seas or cold-water influence moderate temperature and can slow ripening, especially in coastal vineyards.
Soil type influences drainage, heat retention, and vine vigor, shaping the style and consistency of the wines.
humanFactors: Harvest timing, canopy management, and site choice are important tools for keeping balance in warmer areas.
Water management, shade from canopy, and choosing cooler or higher sites are important human responses in Mediterranean climates.
Regional style depends not only on climate and site, but also on deliberate human choices in the vineyard and cellar.
Matching grape varieties and rootstocks to the best sites is an important human factor behind regional wine quality.

abruzzo.name: Abruzzo
abruzzo.summary: Central Adriatic region known for Montepulciano-based reds with ripe fruit and generous body.
abruzzo.styleSummary: Important for Montepulciano-based reds with ripe fruit and good body.
examTips: Montepulciano is the key grape here.
Do not confuse the grape Montepulciano with Vino Nobile di Montepulciano in Tuscany.

Montepulciano.summary: Deep-coloured Italian red grape producing ripe black fruit, body and moderate to firm tannin.
Montepulciano.style: Deep colour, ripe black fruit and medium to high tannin.
viticulture: Performs well in warm central Italy
winemaking: Usually fruit-driven but can be more structured
examTips: Dark fruit and deeper colour are useful clues.
Name confusion is a common exam trap.

vineyardFactors: Warmer conditions increase sugar accumulation and body, so retaining acidity and avoiding over-ripeness become more important.
Mediterranean conditions usually bring abundant sunshine and relatively dry growing seasons, helping consistent ripening.
Altitude or large diurnal range helps preserve acidity and aromatic definition even where sunshine is strong.
humanFactors: Harvest timing, canopy management, and site choice are important tools for keeping balance in warmer areas.
Water management, shade from canopy, and choosing cooler or higher sites are important human responses in Mediterranean climates.
Site selection by elevation and harvest timing are major human choices in these vineyards.
Matching grape varieties and rootstocks to the best sites is an important human factor behind regional wine quality.

campania.name: Campania
campania.summary: Southern Italian region influenced by volcanic soils, producing structured whites and powerful reds.
campania.styleSummary: Structured whites and full-bodied reds influenced by volcanic soils.
examTips: Volcanic soils are a key theme in Campania.
Aglianico is one of Italy’s most structured red grapes.
Southern whites can still show high acidity due to altitude.

Aglianico.summary: Powerful late-ripening red grape producing high tannin, high acidity wines with ageing potential.
Aglianico.style: Dark fruit, high tannin, high acidity, structured and ageworthy.
examTips: High tannin + high acidity is key.
Often compared to Nebbiolo in structure.

Fiano.summary: Textural white grape producing complex wines with floral, nutty and honeyed notes.
Fiano.style: Medium to full-bodied white with floral, honey and nutty notes.
examTips: More texture and weight than many Italian whites.

Greco.summary: Fresh high-acid white grape producing citrus-driven wines with mineral edge.
Greco.style: Fresh citrus white with high acidity and mineral character.
examTips: Higher acidity than many southern Italian whites.

vineyardFactors: Warmer conditions increase sugar accumulation and body, so retaining acidity and avoiding over-ripeness become more important.
Mediterranean conditions usually bring abundant sunshine and relatively dry growing seasons, helping consistent ripening.
Altitude or large diurnal range helps preserve acidity and aromatic definition even where sunshine is strong.
Soil type influences drainage, heat retention, and vine vigor, shaping the style and consistency of the wines.
humanFactors: Harvest timing, canopy management, and site choice are important tools for keeping balance in warmer areas.
Water management, shade from canopy, and choosing cooler or higher sites are important human responses in Mediterranean climates.
Site selection by elevation and harvest timing are major human choices in these vineyards.
Matching grape varieties and rootstocks to the best sites is an important human factor behind regional wine quality.

puglia.name: Puglia
puglia.summary: Hot southern Italian region producing ripe, full-bodied reds, often from high-yielding vineyards.
puglia.styleSummary: Ripe, full-bodied reds with soft acidity.
examTips: Think hot climate and ripe fruit.
Often softer acidity and higher alcohol.
Primitivo is genetically related to Zinfandel.

Primitivo.summary: Early-ripening grape producing high-alcohol, ripe fruit-driven wines.
Primitivo.style: Ripe black fruit, jammy character and high alcohol.
examTips: Think jammy fruit and high alcohol.
Compare with Zinfandel.

Negroamaro.summary: Dark-fruited grape producing medium to full-bodied wines with moderate tannin.
Negroamaro.style: Dark fruit, moderate tannin and slightly bitter finish.
examTips: Often slightly bitter finish is a clue.

vineyardFactors: Warmer conditions increase sugar accumulation and body, so retaining acidity and avoiding over-ripeness become more important.
Mediterranean conditions usually bring abundant sunshine and relatively dry growing seasons, helping consistent ripening.
Nearby seas or cold-water influence moderate temperature and can slow ripening, especially in coastal vineyards.
humanFactors: Harvest timing, canopy management, and site choice are important tools for keeping balance in warmer areas.
Water management, shade from canopy, and choosing cooler or higher sites are important human responses in Mediterranean climates.
Matching grape varieties and rootstocks to the best sites is an important human factor behind regional wine quality.

basilicata.name: Basilicata
basilicata.summary: Small southern region known for structured Aglianico from elevated volcanic sites.
basilicata.styleSummary: Structured reds from Aglianico with freshness from elevation.
examTips: Aglianico from altitude is the key idea.
Think structure, tannin and ageing potential.

Aglianico.summary: Powerful structured red grape producing dark-fruited, high-acid, high-tannin wines with ageing potential.
Aglianico.style: Dark fruit, high tannin and high acidity with ageworthy potential.
examTips: Think southern Italy but with freshness from altitude.
Very ageworthy style.

vineyardFactors: Moderate conditions generally allow steady ripening while still retaining freshness and balance.
Mediterranean conditions usually bring abundant sunshine and relatively dry growing seasons, helping consistent ripening.
Altitude lowers temperature and can extend the growing season, helping preserve acidity and aroma.
Altitude or large diurnal range helps preserve acidity and aromatic definition even where sunshine is strong.
humanFactors: Growers can use site and harvest decisions to balance reliable ripening with freshness and style objectives.

Growers often use elevation and harvest timing strategically to balance ripeness and freshness.
Site selection by elevation and harvest timing are major human choices in these vineyards.

sicily.name: Sicily
sicily.summary: Large Mediterranean island producing ripe wines, with cooler volcanic sites on Mount Etna.
sicily.styleSummary: Ripe wines with regional variation, especially cooler styles on Etna.
examTips: Sicily combines hot climate with cooler volcanic zones.
Etna is key for fresher, more elegant styles.
Nero d’Avola is the main grape to remember.

Nero d’Avola.summary: Main Sicilian red grape producing ripe dark fruit, spice and full-bodied wines.
Nero d’Avola.style: Ripe dark fruit, spice and medium to full body.
examTips: Think ripe fruit and warm climate.
Usually softer than northern Italian reds.

Nerello Mascalese.summary: Volcanic-site red grape producing elegant wines with higher acidity and lighter colour.
Nerello Mascalese.style: Lighter colour, higher acidity, red fruit and mineral notes.
winemaking: Often compared to Pinot Noir or Nebbiolo styles
examTips: Think Etna and higher elevation.
More elegant than Nero d’Avola.

Grillo.summary: Fresh to aromatic white grape used for both dry wines and historically Marsala.
Grillo.style: Fresh citrus and tropical fruit white.
examTips: Often fresher than expected for a hot region.

vineyardFactors: Warmer conditions increase sugar accumulation and body, so retaining acidity and avoiding over-ripeness become more important.
Mediterranean conditions usually bring abundant sunshine and relatively dry growing seasons, helping consistent ripening.
Altitude or large diurnal range helps preserve acidity and aromatic definition even where sunshine is strong.
Nearby seas or cold-water influence moderate temperature and can slow ripening, especially in coastal vineyards.
humanFactors: Harvest timing, canopy management, and site choice are important tools for keeping balance in warmer areas.
Water management, shade from canopy, and choosing cooler or higher sites are important human responses in Mediterranean climates.
Site selection by elevation and harvest timing are major human choices in these vineyards.
Matching grape varieties and rootstocks to the best sites is an important human factor behind regional wine quality.

piemonte.name: Piemonte
piemonte.summary: North-west Italian region known for Nebbiolo-based fine wines, Barbera and aromatic Moscato for Asti.
piemonte.styleSummary: Structured ageworthy reds from Nebbiolo plus fragrant sparkling and sweet Moscato-based wines.
examTips: Nebbiolo gives pale colour but high tannin and high acidity.
Asti and Moscato d’Asti are key sparkling/sweet wines for Level 3.
Barolo and Barbaresco are benchmark Nebbiolo appellations.

Nebbiolo.summary: Classic Piedmont grape producing pale-coloured but high-acid, high-tannin wines with floral and tar notes.
Nebbiolo.style: Rose, red cherry, tar, high acidity and high tannin with long ageing potential.
viticulture: Needs the best sites to ripen fully
winemaking: Oak ageing common in premium styles
examTips: Pale colour does not mean low tannin.
Barolo and Barbaresco are the key benchmark wines.

Barbera.summary: Important red grape producing deeply coloured wines with high acidity and softer tannin than Nebbiolo.
Barbera.style: Juicy red and black fruit, high acidity and moderate tannin.
examTips: Higher acidity and lower tannin than Nebbiolo.
Often more approachable young.

Moscato Bianco.summary: Highly aromatic grape used for Asti and Moscato d’Asti, giving floral grapey wines with low alcohol.
Moscato Bianco.style: Grapey, floral, peachy and sweet with low alcohol and gentle mousse or frizzante.
viticulture: Needs careful handling to preserve aromatics
winemaking: Interrupted fermentation keeps sweetness and low alcohol
examTips: Asti is fully sparkling; Moscato d’Asti is usually lightly sparkling and lower alcohol.
Primary fruit and aromatics matter more than autolytic notes.

Cortese.summary: Fresh high-acid white grape best known for Gavi, producing light to medium-bodied wines with citrus and green apple character.
Cortese.style: Dry, fresh, citrus-led white with green apple and subtle almond notes.
viticulture: Best examples come from carefully sited hillside vineyards
winemaking: Usually made in stainless steel for freshness
examTips: Think Gavi for Cortese.
Usually lighter and fresher than many rich Italian whites.

asti.name: Asti
asti.style: Sweet, fully sparkling, low alcohol and intensely grapey aromatic wine.
asti.keyPoint: Sweet sparkling Moscato-based wine with low alcohol.

moscato-dasti.name: Moscato d’Asti
moscato-dasti.style: Lightly sparkling sweet Moscato with lower pressure and lower alcohol than Asti.
moscato-dasti.aging: Stopped fermentation keeps sweetness and low alcohol
moscato-dasti.keyPoint: Usually frizzante rather than fully sparkling.

barolo.name: Barolo
barolo.summary: Prestige Langhe appellation producing powerful long-lived Nebbiolo wines.
barolo.styleSummary: Full-bodied high-tannin high-acid Nebbiolo with rose, tar and red cherry notes.
examTips: Nebbiolo means pale color but high tannin and acidity.
Barolo is generally the most powerful classic Piemonte expression.

Nebbiolo.summary: Classic Piedmont grape producing pale-coloured but high-acid, high-tannin wines with floral and tar notes.
Nebbiolo.style: Rose, red cherry, tar, high acidity and high tannin with long ageing potential.
viticulture: Needs the best sites to ripen fully
winemaking: Oak ageing common in premium styles
examTips: Pale colour does not mean low tannin.
Barolo and Barbaresco are the key benchmark wines.

barolo.classification: Barolo DOCG is the prestige full-bodied Nebbiolo appellation with long ageing expectations.
vineyardFactors: Moderate conditions generally allow steady ripening while still retaining freshness and balance.
Continental conditions usually mean a wider seasonal and day-night temperature range, with frost and winter cold often relevant.
Slope, aspect, and drainage strongly affect ripening, frost risk, and vineyard quality.
humanFactors: Growers can use site and harvest decisions to balance reliable ripening with freshness and style objectives.
Variety selection, frost protection, and matching the best exposures to the right grapes are important in continental regions.
Terracing, manual work, and careful site matching become more important on steep or exposed slopes.
Choice of the best grape varieties and exact site exposure is an important human factor in this sub-region.

barbaresco.name: Barbaresco
barbaresco.summary: Prestige Nebbiolo appellation producing fragrant but still structured wines from a slightly milder area than Barolo.
barbaresco.styleSummary: Perfumed Nebbiolo with red fruit, floral notes, high acidity and firm tannin.
examTips: Often a little lighter and earlier maturing than Barolo.
Still expect high tannin and acidity.

Nebbiolo.summary: Classic Piedmont grape producing pale-coloured but high-acid, high-tannin wines with floral and tar notes.
Nebbiolo.style: Rose, red cherry, tar, high acidity and high tannin with long ageing potential.
viticulture: Needs the best sites to ripen fully
winemaking: Oak ageing common in premium styles
examTips: Pale colour does not mean low tannin.
Barolo and Barbaresco are the key benchmark wines.

barbaresco.classification: Barbaresco DOCG is also 100% Nebbiolo, usually seen as a little lighter or earlier maturing than Barolo.
vineyardFactors: Moderate conditions generally allow steady ripening while still retaining freshness and balance.
Continental conditions usually mean a wider seasonal and day-night temperature range, with frost and winter cold often relevant.
Slope, aspect, and drainage strongly affect ripening, frost risk, and vineyard quality.
humanFactors: Growers can use site and harvest decisions to balance reliable ripening with freshness and style objectives.
Variety selection, frost protection, and matching the best exposures to the right grapes are important in continental regions.
Terracing, manual work, and careful site matching become more important on steep or exposed slopes.
Choice of the best grape varieties and exact site exposure is an important human factor in this sub-region.

asti.name: Asti
asti.summary: Piemonte sparkling area known for sweet low-alcohol Moscato d’Asti and Asti styles.
asti.styleSummary: Light aromatic sparkling wines with grapey peach and floral notes and often some sweetness.
examTips: Moscato Bianco gives very pronounced grapey aromatics.
Asti and Moscato d’Asti are key Level 3 sparkling styles.

Moscato Bianco.summary: Highly aromatic grape used for Asti and Moscato d’Asti, giving floral grapey wines with low alcohol.
Moscato Bianco.style: Grapey, floral, peachy and sweet with low alcohol and gentle mousse or frizzante.
viticulture: Needs careful handling to preserve aromatics
winemaking: Interrupted fermentation keeps sweetness and low alcohol
examTips: Asti is fully sparkling; Moscato d’Asti is usually lightly sparkling and lower alcohol.
Primary fruit and aromatics matter more than autolytic notes.

asti.classification: Asti DOCG and Moscato d’Asti DOCG are key aromatic sparkling appellations based on Moscato Bianco.
vineyardFactors: Moderate conditions generally allow steady ripening while still retaining freshness and balance.
Continental conditions usually mean a wider seasonal and day-night temperature range, with frost and winter cold often relevant.
Slope, aspect, and drainage strongly affect ripening, frost risk, and vineyard quality.
humanFactors: Growers can use site and harvest decisions to balance reliable ripening with freshness and style objectives.
Variety selection, frost protection, and matching the best exposures to the right grapes are important in continental regions.
Terracing, manual work, and careful site matching become more important on steep or exposed slopes.
Choice of the best grape varieties and exact site exposure is an important human factor in this sub-region.

gavi.name: Gavi
gavi.summary: Important southeastern Piemonte white wine area producing dry Cortese-based wines.
gavi.styleSummary: Dry fresh whites with citrus, apple and almond notes and high acidity.
examTips: Know Gavi as the key Cortese appellation.
Usually a fresh stainless-steel style.

Cortese.summary: Fresh high-acid white grape best known for Gavi, producing light to medium-bodied wines with citrus and green apple character.
Cortese.style: Dry, fresh, citrus-led white with green apple and subtle almond notes.
viticulture: Best examples come from carefully sited hillside vineyards
winemaking: Usually made in stainless steel for freshness
examTips: Think Gavi for Cortese.
Usually lighter and fresher than many rich Italian whites.

gavi.classification: Gavi DOCG is the classic Cortese appellation of southeastern Piemonte.
vineyardFactors: Moderate conditions generally allow steady ripening while still retaining freshness and balance.
Continental conditions usually mean a wider seasonal and day-night temperature range, with frost and winter cold often relevant.
Slope, aspect, and drainage strongly affect ripening, frost risk, and vineyard quality.
humanFactors: Growers can use site and harvest decisions to balance reliable ripening with freshness and style objectives.
Variety selection, frost protection, and matching the best exposures to the right grapes are important in continental regions.
Terracing, manual work, and careful site matching become more important on steep or exposed slopes.
Choice of the best grape varieties and exact site exposure is an important human factor in this sub-region.

piemonte.classification: Piemonte combines major DOCG red appellations such as Barolo and Barbaresco with important sparkling and white appellations such as Asti and Gavi.
lawNotes: Nebbiolo gives pale color but high tannin and acidity.
Asti and Moscato d’Asti are important sweet sparkling exam styles.
vineyardFactors: Moderate conditions generally allow steady ripening while still retaining freshness and balance.
Continental conditions usually mean a wider seasonal and day-night temperature range, with frost and winter cold often relevant.
Slope, aspect, and drainage strongly affect ripening, frost risk, and vineyard quality.
Conditions favoring botrytis or late harvesting can be essential vineyard factors where sweet wines are important.
humanFactors: Growers can use site and harvest decisions to balance reliable ripening with freshness and style objectives.
Variety selection, frost protection, and matching the best exposures to the right grapes are important in continental regions.
Terracing, manual work, and careful site matching become more important on steep or exposed slopes.
Choice of production method and maturation is a major human factor in shaping regional wine style.

examTips: Italy is a key Level 3 country because regional identity matters more than a single national style.
Piemonte, Veneto and Tuscany are especially important anchors.
Remember both still and sparkling styles, especially Prosecco and Asti.
